The phrase "her wound is incurable" (v. 9) refers to the wickedness of the Northern Kingdom, and the statement, "it is come unto Judah," shows that the spiritual sickness had spread to the Southern Kingdom as well. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 120)

What two statements of Job does Elihu respond to next? (34:5-9) - "I am righteous, but God has taken away my justice." - "It profits a man nothing that he should delight in God."
How does Elihu answer Job's complaint that it profits a man nothing to delight in God? (35:4-7) - God is not manipulated by man's actions

Elder Neal A. Maxwell speaking of the need to "consider your ways", said, "May I add several much lesser questions of my own? Some are addressed to all, and some to members as we follow the counsel of an ancient prophet to 'consider [our] ways.' Why is our life-style sometimes blurred and out of focus when we have been told clearly what "manner of men" and women we ought to be? Of course, as…

Surely there is more than coincidence to the similarities in the conclusion of a suffering Job who said, "I have borne chastisement, I will not offend any more" and the revelation given in modern times in which the Lord says to his disciples, "He that will not bear chastisement is not worthy." (Job 34:31 and D&C 136:31.) (Neal A. Maxwell, Things As They Really Are, p.93)

Finally, Elihu commended Job for bearing chastisement though he felt he had not sinned and complimented him for being willing to be taught; but he warned that God's response might not be according to Job's own expectations (Job 34:31-33). Elihu said what the Lord later said, that "Job hath spoken without knowledge" and multiplied "words against God" (Job 34:35-37; 38:2; 40:2; 42:3). (Ellis T.…
